coredata.dc:title: Magnetic CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposites for removal of Brilliant crysel blue dye using FCCD optimization and adsorption characterization
coredata.dc:creator: Rastgordani, Mahdieh
Zolgharnein, Javad
coredata.dc:description: A novel adsorbent CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized through the facile co-precipitation process and used for the removal of Brilliant Cresyl Blue (BCB) dye from aqueous solutions for the first time. FESEM, XRD, EDX, FT − IR, and VSM techniques were used to characterize the prepared nanocomposites. To optimize BCB removal by CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs, a face-centered central composite design (FCCD) with three levels was utilized. The maximum removal percent (93 %) of BCB was obtained at the optimum conditions of initial dye concentration of 221 mg L−1, 0.05 g adsorbent amount, and pH 5.7. The experimental equilibrium data were examined by D-R, Langmuir, Temkin, and Freundlich isotherm models. The adsorption isotherm follows the Langmuir model with a maximum adsorption capacity of about 139.4 mg g−1. Adsorption kinetic data were also considered using pseudo 1st order, Elovich, pseudo 2nd order, and intra-particle diffusion models. Thermodynamic results show that the BCB dye was adsorbed exothermically, and spontaneously.

head.ce:title: Magnetic CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposites for removal of Brilliant crysel blue dye using FCCD optimization and adsorption characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Arak University, Arak, I.R. Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Arak University, Arak, I.R. Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Arak University, Arak, 38156-876, I.R. Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel adsorbent CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized through the facile co-precipitation process and used for the removal of Brilliant Cresyl Blue (BCB) dye from aqueous solutions for the first time. FESEM, XRD, EDX, FT − IR, and VSM techniques were used to characterize the prepared nanocomposites. To optimize BCB removal by CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs, a face-centered central composite design (FCCD) with three levels was utilized. The maximum removal percent (93 %) of BCB was obtained at the optimum conditions of initial dye concentration of 221 mg L−1, 0.05 g adsorbent amount, and pH 5.7. The experimental equilibrium data were examined by D-R, Langmuir, Temkin, and Freundlich isotherm models. The adsorption isotherm follows the Langmuir model with a maximum adsorption capacity of about 139.4 mg g−1. Adsorption kinetic data were also considered using pseudo 1st order, Elovich, pseudo 2nd order, and intra-particle diffusion models. Thermodynamic results show that the BCB dye was adsorbed exothermically, and spontaneously.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, due to the development of industry and civilization, water pollution has become one of the most critical environmental challenges and raised public and global concerns [1]. As a highly toxic, carcinogenic, and non-biodegradable contaminant, dyes are found in large amounts in the effluents of several industries [2,3]. Therefore, the removal of dyes from industrial wastewater before releasing into the environment is very necessary [2,4]. Until now, multiple approaches such as evaporation, oxidation, membrane filtration [5], co-precipitation, photocatalytic reduction [6–8], reverse osmosis, ion exchange, adsorption, coagulation − flocculation, and electrochemical processes are utilized to remove dyestuff [1,2,4]. Adsorption is one of these treatment approaches which it is low in cost [9], highly efficient, convenient to operate, does not produce harmful side products, and is easy to use [10–12]. The removal of contaminants from aqueous solutions has been achieved with a variety of adsorbents [5,12]. For example, activated carbons [13], clay minerals, chitosan/natural zeolites, chelating materials [5], agricultural waste [14], metal–organic Frameworks (MOFs) [15], Layered Double Hydroxides [16], are often utilized to adsorb dyes from water. Nanostructured adsorbents have been developed as strong candidates for the elimination of pollutants from wastewaters because of surface activity, short diffusion paths, high adsorption capacities, and their large specific surface area. A wide variety of nanoparticles are studied, but graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hydroxyapatite (HAp) are of particular interest because of their wide applications [17,18]. Hydroxyapatite (HAp), Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2 was a naturally available calcium phosphate form, which is the main mineral component in hard tissue like teeth and bone [19]. In addition to its bioactivity, hydroxyapatite contains a lot of other special properties as well, including low solubility in water, biocompatibility, availability, high stability under both reducing and oxidizing conditions, and binding affinity, non-immunogenicity, non-toxicity, and easy synthesizability [20–24]. Thus, HAp was being used for diverse applications such as bone repair, dental filling material, drug and gene delivery, tissue engineering, and water treatment [23,25]. HAp has been studied as an adsorbent for removing pollutants because of its ionic exchange property and excellent adsorption capacity [26]. To improve the removal efficiency of dyes, additional modification has often been carried out using inorganic or organic substances to increase HAp's adsorptive tendency [24–27]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene sheets with diameters mainly between 3 and 20 nm, and lateral sizes between 10 and 100 nm in multiple, single, and double layers [27–29]. These materials have unique properties, including high biocompatibility, low toxicity, robust chemical inertness, excellent photostability, and high fluorescent activity and, can be synthesized by low-cost chemical processes [30–32]. GQDs are superior to ordinary carbon materials because of their nanostructured single-layer graphene sheets, which result in a very high specific surface area, making them very sensitive to changes in their environment [33]. Oxygen-containing functional groups at the edge of GQDs such as epoxy (O), hydroxyl (OH), carbonyl (CO), and carboxyl (COOH) showed excellent interaction abilities with metal ions such as Fe2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, and Cu2+ [34]. As a result of the modification of the surface of HAp with GQDs, it was expected that this material can perform novel functions and achieve improved sorption qualities [35]. However, the carbonaceous adsorbents described above had separation issues. Magnetic nanocomposites are widely used in environmental purification due to their large treatment amount, low cost, short treatment time, and fast separation. It was believed that magnetic hydroxyapatite graphene quantum dots (Magnetic-HAp-GQDs), indicating easy separation and high sorption properties by an external magnet without additional centrifugation or filtration, would have better performances in water purification. However, up to now, there was no report of dye adsorption using CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposites [35,36].

Thus, the main objective of this research was to synthesize a new nano-adsorbent based on magnetic cobalt ferrite-HAp and GQD with enhanced dye adsorption capacity in aqueous solutions. In this case, brilliant crysel blue (BCB) is chosen as a model dye since it has high toxicity and is widely used in the pharmaceutical and textile industries. However, BCB can be an environmentally hazardous dye because it accumulates in the environment and causes pollution [36,37]. Thus, we demonstrate that CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite can be utilized as an efficient adsorbent to remove Brilliant Crysel Blue (BCB) dye from aqueous solutions for the first time. In the present study, the Response Surface Methodology (RSM) was used to optimize the various factors that influence dye adsorption [38–44]. The optimal process conditions were determined using a three-variable Face Centered Central Composite design (FCCD). As well as this, a detailed study was conducted to investigate the adsorption equilibrium process using different adsorption kinetics, thermodynamic analysis to determine the controlling mechanism, and also using isotherm models to study the BCB dye adsorption on CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs

All of the used chemicals Cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2.6H2O 98 %), Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O, 98 %), Calcium chloride (CaCl2.4H2O), Citric acid (C6H8O7), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), ammonium aqueous solution (NH4OH, 25 %), sodium hydroxide, Nitric acid (HNO3), and Brilliant crysel blue dye were provided from Merck (Merck, Darmstadt, Germany). The chemical structure of Brilliant crysel blue (BCB) was given in Scheme 1 (molecular formula of dye was C17H21ClN4O, and molar weight of 332.84 g mol−1 and λmax, BCB: 626 nm). Since all of these compounds were analytical reagent grade, no purification was necessary. To determine the dye concentrations in aqueous solutions, the Analytik Jena SPEC ORD 250 spectrophotometer was used with a quartz cell of diameter 10 cm. The pH was adjusted by using a calibrated digital Metrohm pH meter (Model 691) equipped with a glass electrode.

As described in Scheme 2 , CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanoparticles were synthesized by a two − step method. In the beginning, GQDs were prepared by the same method previously described [33]. A vial containing 0.2 g of citric acid was heated for 10 minutes in a paraffin oil bath at 200 °C. In this period, the powdered citric acid turned into a viscous dark orange liquid. While vigorously stirring at room temperature, this orange liquid was added dropwise to 20 mL of 0.25 mol L−1 NaOH solution. Aqueous GQD solution obtained was stirred for 30 minutes, neutralized with nitric acid to pH 7, and stored at 4 °C in an amber bottle. Then, FeCl3·6H2O (5 g) and CoCl2.6H2O (2.3 g) were completely dissolved in 100 mL deionized water (Fe3+/Co2+ molar ratio = 2). The 25 % NH4OH solution (20 mL) was slowly and simultaneously added to this solution using a paraffin oil bath at 80 °C for 30 minutes with vigorous stirring in a nitrogen atmosphere. This step produced a colloidal solution. The solution was then mixed with 10 mL Ca(Cl)2·4H2O solution (0.835 mol L−1), at the same time, the pH of the mixture was kept at 11 by using 10 mL ammonia (NH4OH 25 % w/v). After that, 10 mL of H3PO4 (0.5 mol L−1) was added drop by drop (The molar ratio of Ca/P was approximately 1.7). Following incubation at 80 °C for 30 min, GQDs (20 mL) were added to the mixture and stirred at 80 °C for 30 min. In this way, the precipitated product was cooled at room temperature without stirring and then aged for 2 h. The precipitate was magnetically collected, repeatedly washed with distilled water till reached neutrality, and finally dried at 90 °C for 6 hours.

By using the Galaxy series 5000 FT-IR spectrometer (UNICAM), the functional groups of nanocomposites (CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs) were identified over the range 50–5000 cm−1. Utilizing the X PERTPRO X − ray diffractometer (Philips, Model PW304/60) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å), the degree of crystalline and amorphous structure of the CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs was analyzed in the angular range from 20° to 80° 2θ. The elemental composition and surface morphology of the as-synthesized particles were analyzed by MIRA3 TESCAN Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM). At room temperature, the magnetic measurements were performed with a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM 3886 Kashan, Iran). By using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), the elemental composition of the nanocomposites was determined (Philips FEI 200). A Zeta potential test was carried out on the synthesized nanocomposites using Zeta-sizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) by electrophoretic light scattering (ELS). Ultrasound prob (UP400S-Hielscher) was used for dispersing nanocomposites before the zeta potential analysis for 3 minutes.

A batch equilibrium method was used to conduct the adsorption experiments. The stock solution of Brilliant crysel blue (BCB) dye with a concentration of 1000 mg L−1 was obtained by dissolving an appropriate amount of BCB dye powders in doubly distilled water. A stock solution of dye (1000 mg L−1) was diluted as needed to produce the desired concentration of BCB. A typical run would use 10 mL of fresh BCB solution and add a known amount of nanocomposite sorbent. The pH of the solution was adjusted by the addition of the required amount of 0.1 M NaOH or 0.1 M HNO3 by using pH meter, before adding the adsorbent. The mixture was shaken at 300 rpm (IKA − OS2 Basic Orbital Shaker, Germany) at room temperature for 45 min. In order to check the photocatalytic effects of current synthesized adsorbent, all experiments were conducted in dark and room ambient conditions, and results showed that there is no light interference in adsorption reactions. However, all experimental runs were performed in the absence of light.

The adsorbent was then separated from the solution by using an external magnet. To determine the residual concentration of BCB dye in the experimental solution, its absorbance at λmax 626 nm was measured spectrophotometrically. Eqs. (1) and (2) were used to calculate the dye adsorbed per unit mass of nanocomposite (q) and the dye removal efficiency (%R), respectively [38,39]:

m is the weight of CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs in grams. V is the volume of BCB solution. C0 and Ce are the initial and equilibrium dye concentrations in the solution, respectively, with units of mg L−1.

For modeling the independent variables and the responses from pre-designed experimental data, Response Surface Modeling (RSM) can be used [37,38]. It was possible to determine the optimal set of operational factors in this way. Through the use of statistically designed experiments, the time, the variability, and the total cost of the process can be reduced, thereby improving the quality of the optimization output [37,40,41]. Among the response surface methodology (RSM), the face-centered central composite design (FCCD) was one of the most frequently used. It consists of three parts: (1) a full factorial or fractional factorial design (2 k); (2) a star design (2 k axial points), in which experimental runs are situated at a distance α from its center (In FCCD, the star points are located at the center of each face of the factorial space, so α = ± 1); and (3) replicates at the center points Cp, where k was the number of factors [42]. Multiple center points were used to have substantial degrees of freedom for estimating pure error. Therefore, the total number of experiments (N) in FCCD equals to N = 2 k + 2 k + Cp. In this study, there are three factors (k = 3) with three levels low (−1), high (+1), and center (0)), so the number of needed experiments was 20. According to Eq. (3), the factor (Xi ) was coded as xi for statistical calculations [39,43].

βo, βij, βi, βii, and y represent the mean, interaction, first-order, quadratic coefficients, and predicted response, respectively. In addition, xj and xi show the coded values of the independent factors, and ε is the random error, while k is the number of factors. Table 2 shows the coded values and their levels of the effective factors. By using Minitab software Version 17, the experimental design data were analyzed and validation of the obtained model was checked by applying analysis of variance.

The X-ray diffractograms of CoFe2O4, CoFe2O4@HAP, and CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs are depicted in Fig. 1 A. Figure .1A shows the main peaks corresponding to CoFe2O4 nanoparticles at (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440). These peaks were located at 2θ = 30.22°, 36°, 43.55°, 54.16°, 57.54°, and 62.58°, respectively. They are well indexed to the pure CoFe2O4 with a cubic spinel structure (space group: Fd3m (227), JCPDS no. 89-0691) which has been previously reported in the literature (Fig. 1A) [45]. Moreover, the pattern was free of peaks related to CoO and hematite (Fe2O3), which confirmed the excellent purity of the nanoparticles. Also, XRD patterns for CoFe2O4@HAp and CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs (Fig. 1(A)) revealed that the intensity of diffraction peaks at 30.1°, 35.5°, 53.5°, 57.0°, and 62.6° decreased, and some peaks vanished (43.1°) because both HAps and GQDs formed obstructions in the crystal plane of CoFe2O4. Thus, the results of the XRD confirmed the presence of both HAp and GQDs composites on the CoFe2O4 spinel without causing significant damage to its crystal structure.

According to the EDX analysis of the prepared CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite, it contains many elements such as Fe, Co, O, Ca, P, and C, and except for the mentioned elements, there are no other impurities in this nanocomposite, which confirms the successful synthesis of nanoparticles (Fig. 1B). A ratio of Ca:P of 1.58 was determined from the EDX, which corresponded to the stoichiometric ratio (1.67), which exists naturally in the HAp [20]. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope micrographs of the nanoparticles were obtained using an accelerating voltage of 100 kV (Fig. 1D). The morphology of the magnetic CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite can be characterized as a spherical and uniform distribution of nano-particles aggregated on each other, with particle sizes ranging between 16 and 30 nm. The elemental composition of CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite was also confirmed using EDX measurements. With this process, the CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite could be prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Infrared spectrum analysis and Zeta protentional
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR was employed to characterize the surface functional groups and chemical structure of GQDs, CoFe2O4, CoFe2O4-HAp, and CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite before and after adsorption (in the range of 400 − 4000 cm−1). As shown in Fig. 2 b, the peak in 585 cm−1 was due to stretching vibrations of Fe3+–O2− in Fe3O4 [44] . The broad peak at 3413 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching frequencies of the hydroxyl (OH) groups in FeOH moieties (Fig.2b). The observed band at 928 cm−1 was assigned to the CoO group. The bands at 928 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 were ascribed to CoO and OH groups, respectively [19,46,47]. The spectrum of CoFe2O4@HAp (Fig.2c) presented new strong bands at 1034 and 566 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching modes of the OPO bonds of PO4 3− in HAp [19]. The small broad peak at 1545 cm−1 was characteristic of NH4 + group and was related to the NH4OH 28 % (v/v) used in the CoFe2O4@HAp synthesis, which remains in the adsorbent powder despite repeated washing with deionized water. The formation of GQDs is demonstrated by the absorption of carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups by GQDs. These show that nanomaterials contain -COOH groups. While the symmetric and asymmetric -COO- groups of graphene quantum dots appeared at 1440 cm−1 and 1611 cm−1 (Fig. 2a) [48]. The spectrum of CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs has a wide interference band; however, the interference band is reduced in the trace (Fig. 2d). As shown in spectrum 2d, the peaks at 1622 and 1092 cm−1 were attributed to GQDs due to CO stretching vibration and CO (alkoxy) stretch band respectively. This confirms the formation of the CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nano-composite [33]. Comparing spectra d with e in Fig. 2, shows the shift of peaks from 1092 cm−1 to 1076 cm−1, 1622 cm−1 To 1600 cm−1, and 3413 cm−1 to 3381 cm−1 support the chemisorption of dye molecules. Spectrum 2e illustrates dye molecules having interaction with nanocomposite adsorbent through the appearance of new peaks, displacement, or changes in peak intensity. Additionally, zeta potential measurement (−13.37 mV) confirmed the negatively charged nature of GQDs surfaces. A consistent result from FTIR and zeta potential measurements showed that GQDs had negative charges in zeta potential measurements because they contained numerous hydroxyl and carboxyl groups.

The magnetization curves of CoFe2O4, CoFe2O4@HAp, and CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs, recorded at room temperature, are shown in Fig. 1C. These results demonstrate that the materials as prepared are magnetic and that the maximum saturation magnetization (MS) value of CoFe2O4 is 53.14 (emu g−1), that of CoFe2O4@HAp is 38.1 (emu g−1) and that of CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs is 32.23 (emu g−1). The decrease in saturation magnetization value of CoFe2O4@HAp and CoFe2O4@HAP-GQDs than to CoFe2O4 was attributed to the introduction of non-magnetic HAp and GQDs in the nanocomposites. The magnetic hysteresis curve showed that CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs was a superparamagnetic material and showed sufficient magnetism for practical applications. An external magnetic field caused the well-dispersed CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs to separate from the aqueous solution within a very short period of time after adsorption. In addition to facilitating post-processing, lowering costs, and reducing risks, this fast magnetic separation of nano-adsorbent was extremely advantageous [44–47].

A face-centered central composite design (FCCD) for BCB dye removal experiments, was utilized, and the RSM approach was used to identify the optimum conditions. For BCB dye removal experiments, three independent factors, including pH, the initial concentration of BCB dye, and adsorbent dosage (m) (as the independent variables) were defined, and their effects on dye removal efficiency (%R) as the response (or dependent variable) were evaluated. The experimental levels and ranges of effective factors for matrix design are listed in Table 1
. This Table also shows the resulting FCCD matrix of the factors and contains estimates of the responses. Based on the FCCD experimental results, a full second − order polynomial equation fitted well with the multiple regression analysis, which explained the relationship between dye removal efficiency and effective variables as well as their interactions. In Eq. 5 it is represented the final empirical equation in terms of coded factors:

As described above, the %R is the BCB dye removal percent predicted by the equation. Analysis of variance (ANOVA), coefficient of determination (R2), and analysis of residuals were utilized for proving the adequacy and validity of the represented model. The results of the ANOVA test, along with regression coefficient values of the suggested 2nd order equation (Eq. 5) have been displayed in Table 2. As can be seen the regression of model with a 95 % confidence level (p < 0.05) is significant. In running ANOVA, Fisher's F-test results show the larger F − values and the smaller p-values which indicates that the model has more significant terms [39,43,49]. Furthermore, the lack of fit test was able to determine how well the obtained model fits the experimental data. In Table 2, p > 0.05 indicates a non-significant lack of fit of the proposed quadratic model. The p-values listed in Table 2 show that there was no evidence of lack of fit, and variation can be accounted by pure error. The coefficients of determination (R2) and the adjusted coefficients of determination (R2 adj) can also be used for assessing the goodness − of − fit of this model. When R2 was close to 1, experimental data and the model are more likely to agree. As illustrated in Table 2, the coefficients of determination in this study were very close to 1 (R2 = 0.9992), indicating that only around 0.08 % of the total variation could not be explained by the regression model (Eq. 5). The R2 values and the adjusted R2 adj values support both the acceptability and rationality of the sample size and the number of terms in the model (R2 adj = 0.9988). Further, the values of R2 were quite close to the predicted R2 (R2 (Pred)%R = 0.9973), so it can be concluded that the model was able to describe perfectly the range of factors studied and that the removal percent of BCB dye could also be accurately predicted. Figs. 3 A and 3B are shown the main effects of the factors and the interactions between them on the adsorption of BCB onto CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite. The pH of the solution and adsorbent mass have positive effects, but BCB concentration has an adverse impact on the removal percent (Fig. 3A). An adsorbent with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, such as CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs, was able to act as an anion even at mildly acidic pH levels (i.e., pH 5), which perhaps it accounts for the high removal of BCB dye. Because of the competition between H+ and the cationic dye molecules anchored at adsorption sites, BCB dye exhibited lower adsorption capacities at lower solution pH (pH = 2). The normal probability plot of residuals showed the data points are fairly close to a straight line which means that residuals are normally distributed (Fig .4A). Fig. 4 B clearly shows a good correlation between the actual and predicted values of Y (R%) for the adsorption of BCB onto CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite. Fig. 4B, it is evident that the developed model was robust because the residuals for the prediction for the majority of responses are less than 10 %, and the residuals tend to be close to the diagonal line [39,43]. Figs. 4C and 4D represent the effects of independent factors and their interactions on dye removal as three-dimensional response surfaces (3D) and two − dimensional contour plots (2D). For a particular pair of factors, the plots were made at the median values of other factors. It was important to consider interactions between independent factors in contour plots since high interactions mean maximum, minimum, or saddle points on the surface of the response, which helps to estimate the optimization process. The removal efficiency may be limited by increasing BCB concentration as shown in Fig. 4C. As a result of the progressive adsorption process, nanocomposite sites are highly saturated. By increasing the amount of adsorbent to 0.06 g, the removal efficiency increased and then remained constant. As well, the contour lines in Fig. 4D are elliptical, suggesting that there was a significant interaction between the adsorbent dosage and dye concentration. To reach the maximum removal efficiency, the final goal of the optimization task was to determine the optimal values for the factors in the experimentation region. In the proposed model, maximum removal efficiency was found to be 93 % under pH = 5.7, adsorbent dose (m) = 0.05 g, and Initial dye concentration (C) = 221 mg L−1.

To understand the adsorption mechanism onto the prepared nanocomposite, the kinetics of adsorption are investigated. Pseudo 1st order, pseudo 2nd order, intra-particle diffusion, and Elovich equation are commonly used to describe the adsorption kinetics on CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs (Eqs. (6-9)), respectively) [38,50,51]. A linear curve-fitting procedure was used to estimate the parameters for the kinetic models based on experimental data.

As, qe and qt (mg g−1) indicate the adsorption capacity of BCB at equilibrium and time, K1 (min−1) indicates the constant rate of first-order, and K2 (g (mg. min)−1) indicates the constant rate of second-order. In the Elovich model (Eq. (9)), β is the desorption constant during the experiment, and α (mg g−1 min−1) is initial adsorption rate. ki (mg min−0.5 g−1) is the rate constant of intra-particle diffusion, and Ci is the thickness of the boundary layer [38,39,50,51]. The kinetic parameters of all the above models were represented in Table 3 . A model's validity was assessed by its experimental and calculated data, as well as by its coefficient of determination (R2). In the case of the pseudo 2nd order model (Fig. 5 A), the R2 is above 0.9917, and the error values are less than a unit. The calculated qe value (qe, Cal = 47.3 mg g−1) obtained from the pseudo 2nd order model was closer to the experimental qe (qe, Exp = 42.2 mg g−1) than the one obtained from the pseudo 1st order model, indicating that the pseudo 2nd order model was well adapted to the experimental data. The Weber-Morris intra-particle diffusion model was also studied to better understand BCB dye adsorption mechanism on CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs. The amounts of intercept C and kid were calculated from the plot of t0.5 versus qt and were recorded in Table 3. In Fig. 5.B, it can be seen that the plots of qt and t0.5 do not pass through the origin, indicating that internal diffusion was not merely a rate-controlling step. The kid was found to increase from 7.37 to 9.36 mg (g min0.5)−1, and the constant C increased from 1.55 to 2.53 mg/g with the increasing of BCB initial concentration from 200 to 300 mg L−1. According to these results, increasing BCB concentration causes decreasing diffusion in the boundary layer and increasing diffusion in the adsorbent [39]. Table 3, shows that the diffusion rate constant values in the second stage (Ki2) were lower than those in the first stage (Ki1), suggesting that the second stage might be the rate − limiting step since it was slower than the first stage [52].

Elovich equation Eq. (9) was commonly used in delineating a chemisorption process, and it was successfully applied in adsorbing BCB from an aqueous solution. A plot of qt versus ln(t) should yield a straight line with a slope of (1/β) and an intercept of ln(αβ) [52]. Table 3 gives also the Elovich constants determined by the slope and intercept of the straight line.

In the empirical Langmuir isotherm model, monolayer adsorption is assumed to occur at a finite number of localized sites, and all sites are equally attachable to the dye [38,39,53]. The Langmuir equation can be expressed as follows [38,51,53,54]:

(mg g−1). In addition, b was Langmuir's isotherm constant (L mg−1); it was a measure of the affinity between dye and adsorbent molecules, which has a direct relation to the free energy of adsorption [38,39,43]. The values of the qmax, b constants, and the coefficient of determinations are given in Table 4 and Fig. 5 (A and C). In addition, RL was calculated with respect to the Langmuir model and it was defined as follows:

At the highest concentration studied, 2000 mg L−1, the RL value for BCB adsorption on CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs is 0.046, while at the lowest concentration 50 mg L−1, this value is 0.99. It represents that the BCB uptake process seems to be desirable.

The Freundlich isotherm, in contrast, relies on the assumption that there was multilayer adsorption on a heterogeneous surface [55].

In the above equations, Kf represents Freundlich isotherm constant (mg g−1) (L mg−1)1/n. 1/n represents the intensity of adsorption, and it was a measure of the desirability of the process (Fig. 6 A and B). Moreover, the Freundlich constant (1/n) was commonly used to determine whether the adsorption process is favorable. Since the 1/n value was less than 1 the adsorption process was favorable (Table 4).

According to the Temkin isotherm, the heat of adsorption decreases linearly with increasing molecule coverage. Furthermore, the adsorption was characterized by a uniform distribution of binding energies, up to maximum binding strength. It was applied according to Eq. (18) [56]:

The Temkin constant, B = RT/b, was derived from the heat of adsorption, A (L g−1), the equilibrium binding constant, T (K) the absolute temperature, and R (8.314 J (mol K)−1) the universal gas constant [53].

To differentiate between the physical and chemical adsorption by the nanocomposite, the adsorption data were further analyzed using a Dubinin − Radushkevich isotherm model. It can be expressed as follows [57,58]:

This equation states that qe (mol g−1) is the equilibrium adsorption capacity, qDR (mol g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity. β (mol2 kJ−2) is an activity coefficient based on the mean free energy of adsorption, and ε is the Polanyi potential, which defined as [43,51]:

R refers to the gas constant, 8.314 J (mol K)−1, and T denotes the temperature (K). The equilibrium data fit the Dubinin − Radushkevich isotherm model well by plotting ln qe against ε2. The intercept and slope of the fitted line can be used to calculate qDR and β (Fig. 6C). The mean free energy of adsorption is given by:
(23)

If 8 kJ mol−1 < E < 16 kJ mol−1, then chemical adsorption dominates and when E < 8 kJ mol−1, physical adsorption governs. The Dubinin − Radushkevich equation parameters are illustrated in Table 4. According to obtained E = 15.8 kJ mol−1 value, the adsorption of BCB was due to chemisorption. The four isotherm models are fitted with linear and nonlinear equations and shown in Fig. 6. The values of the fitted isotherm constants and errors from linear and nonlinear methods are also given in Table 4 for various models. By comparing the coefficient of determinations (R2), χ2, and Δq, it can be suggested that the Langmuir isotherm model better fits the experimental data (R2 > 0.99, χ2 = 3.23, and Δq = 0.072). Furthermore, the qmax values obtained by the Langmuir model were close to the experimental capacity uptake (qm,exp), conformed that this model fit to the data well. So, the equilibrium adsorption capacity of CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs for BCB dye reaches 136 mg g−1 at 298 K. Therefore, based on the Langmuir model assumptions, it can be concluded that the uptake of BCB dye onto CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs was monolayer and homogeneous on the nanocomposite surface.

Using the following equations, thermodynamic parameters can be evaluated, such as a change in entropy (ΔS°), a change in enthalpy (ΔH°), and a change in free energy (ΔG°) [38,39,51].

Cad (mg L−1) represents the amount of dye adsorbed in equilibrium, Ce (mg L−1) represents the equilibrium concentration in solution, and Kc represents the equilibrium constant. Also, R is equal to 8.314 J mol−1 K−1, where T is the absolute temperature (K°). Fig. 7 shows the Van't Hoff plot of Kc versus 1/T, which gives the slope and intercept of ΔH° and ΔS° [38,39,43]. Table 5 presents the calculated values for each thermodynamic parameter at different temperatures. According to equation (26), for each temperature range studied, all ΔG° values were negative, indicating that the adsorption of BCB dye onto CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite is spontaneous and feasible (Table 5). The enthalpy value for BCB dye (-11 kJ mol−1) illustrated that the adsorption process was exothermic, resulting in a decrease in adsorption with increasing temperature. As the temperature increases from 298 to 328 K, the enthalpy decreases for two reasons: (i) with an increase in temperature, hydrogen bonding will decline. ii) increased mobility at high temperatures will not assist BCB molecules in entering the CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs [59,60]. The negative entropy (ΔS°= −33.3 J (mol K)−1) was implied that BCB adsorption on CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs abated stochastic distribution at the solid-liquid interface [58].

The proposed mechanism of BCB dye adsorption on CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs is illustrated in Scheme 3 . As a result of the presence of GQDs, the surface area was increased, as well as oxygen-containing groups were more readily accessible. The mechanism is mainly driven by electrostatic interactions, π-π interactions, as well as hydrogen bonding. Dye molecules and GQDs exhibit electrostatic interactions because of their different surface charges. The π-π interactions are caused by the interaction between the π electrons in BCB dye and the graphitic structure in GQDs. In GQDs, hydrogen atoms interact with N and O atoms in BCB dye to form hydrogen bonds [48,61]. Since the zeta potential of the GQDs is -13.37 mV at pH = 5.7, the surface of the GQDs is negatively charged, promoting electrostatic interactions with cationic dye molecules (BCB) [62]. At lower pH, nanocomposites have low dye adsorption capacities due to the protonation of GQD -COO- surfaces, resulting in an antagonistic interaction [63]. This is a double confirmation of the mechanism that is most likely a chemisorption interaction.

Reusability is defined as the regeneration of an adsorbent from the dye-adsorbed condition and its implications for dye adsorption [3,64]. From an economic perspective, it is crucial that the adsorbents are reusable. In four successful cycles of BCB removal, CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposites produce a 78 % removal efficiency under optimal conditions. A solution of BCB at 221 mg L−1 pH = 5.7 was prepared with distilled and tap water to evaluate the adsorbent efficiency in real samples. 0.05 g of adsorbent was added to each solution. After passing 35 min, the percentage of BCB removal for distilled and tap water were 93 % and 80 % respectively [3,64–67].

A simple solvothermal method was used to prepare CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposites, which were then used for removal of BCB dye from water. The FCCD was used to determine a suitable model for related of removal percent with effective factors and optimize conditions. The optimum adsorption conditions were assigned as sorbent dose 0.05 g, initial concentration of BCB 221 mg L−1, and pH 5.7. Under this condition, the maximum BCB removal percent(%R) reached 93 %. The Freundlich, Temkin, D − R, and Langmuir, isotherm models were used to consider the adsorption equilibrium behavior of CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs nanocomposite for BCB dye removal. Equilibrium data for adsorption of BCB dye on CoFe2O4@HAp-GQDs could be well fitted by the Langmuir isotherm model, which suggested that adsorption was homogeneous monolayer. Conventional kinetic models were examined, and a pseudo − 2nd − order equation was found to better fit with empirical data. Based on the thermodynamic results, BCB adsorption was spontaneous (ΔG° < 0), and exothermic (ΔH° < 0).
Mahdieh Rastgordani: Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Javad Zolgharnein: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision.
